questions.text: Which planet is known as the 'Red Planet'?
questions.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet because its surface contains iron oxide (rust), giving it a reddish appearance.

questions.text: Which is the hottest planet in the Solar System?
questions.explanation: Venus is the hottest planet (~465°C) due to a runaway greenhouse effect with dense CO₂ atmosphere, even though Mercury is closer to the Sun.

questions.text: The boundary between the crust and the mantle is known as:
questions.explanation: The Mohorovičić (Moho) Discontinuity separates Earth's crust from the mantle, located at ~35 km depth under continents.

questions.text: Which layer of the Earth is in a liquid state?
questions.explanation: The outer core (2,900–5,100 km depth) is liquid iron-nickel. This liquid outer core generates Earth's magnetic field. The inner core is solid despite higher temperatures due to enormous pressure.

questions.text: Which moon of the Solar System has the thickest atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is the only moon in the Solar System with a thick atmosphere — denser than Earth's — composed mainly of nitrogen.

questions.text: A light-year is a unit of:
questions.explanation: A light-year is the distance light travels in one year ≈ 9.46 × 10¹² km. It is purely a unit of distance, not time, despite the name.

questions.text: Which statement about Pluto is correct after the 2006 IAU decision?
options: Pluto is a moon of Neptune
questions.explanation: In 2006, the International Astronomical Union redefined planet criteria. Pluto was reclassified as a 'dwarf planet' because it has not cleared its orbital neighbourhood. The Solar System now has 8 planets.

questions.text: Sunspots are associated with which layer of the Sun?
questions.explanation: Sunspots are cooler, darker regions on the Sun's photosphere (surface layer, ~5,500°C). They appear dark because they are about 1,500°C cooler than surrounding regions. They follow an 11-year cycle.

questions.text: What is the equatorial radius of Earth approximately?
questions.explanation: Earth's equatorial radius is ~6,378 km, while the polar radius is ~6,357 km. Earth is an oblate spheroid — slightly flattened at poles and bulging at the equator.

options: Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned
Sun and Moon are at right angles to Earth
questions.explanation: Spring tides (highest high tides, lowest low tides) occur at full moon and new moon when Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned, creating maximum combined gravitational pull. They are NOT related to the spring season.

questions.text: Which is the nearest star to Earth after the Sun?
questions.explanation: Proxima Centauri (4.24 light-years away) is the nearest star to Earth after the Sun. Sirius is the brightest star in the night sky but not the nearest. Alpha Centauri A is 4.37 light-years away.

questions.text: The Aurora Borealis (Northern Lights) is caused by:
options: Reflection of sunlight on ice crystals
Solar wind particles interacting with Earth's magnetic field
Refraction of moonlight in polar atmosphere
questions.explanation: Auroras (Borealis in North, Australis in South) are caused by charged particles from the solar wind being channelled by Earth's magnetic field into the polar atmosphere, where they excite gas molecules that then emit coloured light.

questions.text: Which planet has the most moons in the Solar System?
questions.explanation: Jupiter has the most confirmed moons (95 as of recent counts), followed by Saturn (146). However, the count fluctuates as new small moons are discovered — check the year of the question. Saturn has historically been listed second.

questions.text: Which planet is least dense in the Solar System?
questions.explanation: Saturn is the least dense planet — its average density (0.687 g/cm³) is less than water (1 g/cm³), meaning it would float if placed in a large enough body of water. Jupiter is the largest but not the least dense.

questions.text: The Challenger Deep, the deepest point on Earth, is located in which ocean?
questions.explanation: Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench (~10,994 m) is the deepest point on Earth's surface, located in the western Pacific Ocean. It was named after HMS Challenger, which first measured it in 1875.

questions.text: Which planet rotates on its axis in a retrograde (east-to-west) direction, similar to Venus?
questions.explanation: Uranus rotates retrograde AND on its side (axial tilt 97.8°). Venus also rotates retrograde (east to west) but nearly upright. Both Venus and Uranus have retrograde rotation, but Uranus is the one that 'rolls' on its side.
